Net rune balance for talon-sealable rectangles

A "talon plate" is a fixed concave polyomino on the square grid that covers exactly $6$ unit squares and has a unique $1$-square concave notch ("pocket") adjacent (by edge) to three of its covered squares. You may place congruent copies of the talon by translations, rotations, and reflections. A placement covers its $6$ squares; the pocket square of that placement is not covered by that same tile.

Call an $m\times n$ rectangle *sealable* if it can be covered perfectly by talon plates (no overlaps, no gaps).

You are given the following candidate rectangles ("chambers"), each with an associated integer rune:

- $21\times28$ has rune $52$
- $24\times25$ has rune $58$
- $42\times26$ has rune $61$
- $25\times28$ has rune $74$
- $32\times33$ has rune $83$
- $35\times24$ has rune $95$
- $54\times38$ has rune $68$
- $44\times27$ has rune $79$

Define the net rune balance
$$
S=(\text{sum of runes of sealable chambers})-(\text{sum of runes of nonsealable chambers}).
$$
Compute $S$.

Solution.
Step 1: Forced-pair invariant implies area divisible by $12$.

For this talon, the pocket structure defines a deterministic map $f$ on tiles in any perfect tiling. For each tile $H$, let $P(H)$ be its pocket square. In a perfect tiling, $P(H)$ is covered by exactly one other tile, denoted $f(H)$. Local forcing implies $f$ is a fixed-point-free involution, so tiles occur in disjoint linked pairs $\{H,f(H)\}$. Each linked pair covers $2\cdot6=12$ squares. Hence a necessary condition for sealability is
$$
12\mid mn.
$$

Step 2: Mod-$4$ obstruction.

For this talon, an additional invariant holds: if
$$
m\equiv n\equiv2\pmod4,
$$
then no tiling exists. This follows from a parity contradiction arising from two independent $4$-periodic colorings applied to all possible linked-pair shapes.

Step 3: Available constructions.

We may use the following standard constructions.

(a) $3\times4$ block: two talons tile a $3\times4$ rectangle. Hence any rectangle whose side lengths are divisible by $3$ and $4$ (in some order) is sealable.

(b) $12\times t$ strips: both $12\times3$ and $12\times4$ rectangles are sealable by $3\times4$ blocks. Therefore any $12\times t$ rectangle is sealable whenever $t=3a+4b$ for some $a,b\ge0$.

Step 4: Coin fact for $3$ and $4$.

Every integer $t\ge6$ can be written as $3a+4b$. Hence every $12\times t$ rectangle with $t\ge6$ is sealable.

Step 5: Classify each candidate.

(i) $21\times28$, rune $52$.

Area $=588=12\cdot49$, passes Step 1. Mod $4$: $21\equiv1$, $28\equiv0$, not forbidden. Since $21$ is divisible by $3$ and $28$ by $4$, a $3\times4$ block tiling applies. Sealable.

(ii) $24\times25$, rune $58$.

Area $=600=12\cdot50$, passes Step 1. Mod $4$: $24\equiv0$, not forbidden. Split into two $12\times25$ strips. Since $25=3\cdot3+4\cdot4$, Step 4 applies. Sealable.

(iii) $42\times26$, rune $61$.

Area $=1092=12\cdot91$, passes Step 1. Mod $4$: $42\equiv2$, $26\equiv2$. Forbidden by Step 2. Not sealable.

(iv) $25\times28$, rune $74$.

Area $=700$, not divisible by $12$. Fails Step 1. Not sealable.

(v) $32\times33$, rune $83$.

Area $=1056=12\cdot88$, passes Step 1. Mod $4$: $32\equiv0$. Since $32$ divisible by $4$ and $33$ by $3$, a $3\times4$ block tiling applies. Sealable.

(vi) $35\times24$, rune $95$.

Area $=840=12\cdot70$, passes Step 1. Mod $4$: $24\equiv0$. Split into two $12\times35$ strips. Since $35=3\cdot1+4\cdot8$, Step 4 applies. Sealable.

(vii) $54\times38$, rune $68$.

Area $=2052=12\cdot171$, passes Step 1. Mod $4$: $54\equiv2$, $38\equiv2$. Forbidden by Step 2. Not sealable.

(viii) $44\times27$, rune $79$.

Area $=1188=12\cdot99$, passes Step 1. Mod $4$: $44\equiv0$. Since $44$ divisible by $4$ and $27$ by $3$, a $3\times4$ block tiling applies. Sealable.

Step 6: Compute the net rune balance.

Sealable runes:
$$
52+58+83+95+79=367.
$$
Nonsealable runes:
$$
61+74+68=203.
$$
Thus
$$
S=367-203=164.
$$

Uniqueness Test

The linked-pair involution forces $12\mid mn$ for any sealable rectangle, uniquely ruling out candidates failing this condition. The mod-$4$ obstruction deterministically excludes rectangles with $m\equiv n\equiv2\pmod4$. For all remaining candidates, explicit constructions certify sealability. Hence each chamber is uniquely classified as sealable or nonsealable, and the resulting signed rune sum is uniquely determined.

164
